artifacts.content:
Sarah,

Just wrapped up another long Sunday of shuffling spreadsheets around and trying to make the 2026–2030 CIP feel like it matches the reality in the garage. I wanted to give you a little more context on why I’ve been so wrapped up in this Unit #23 replacement and why it’s bleeding into home life the way it has.

I keep thinking back to that stretch from January–March 2024 when 23 was down more than it was up. Those storms where I basically lived at the Brainerd Public Works garage meant I missed several of Emily’s anxiety therapy appointments at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic. She’s been seeing that therapist there since October 2023 for the panic attacks, and instead of sitting in the waiting room with you two, I was out chasing hydraulic leaks and trying to reassign routes.

It’s the same story with your stuff. When 23 kept dying during the February 2024 blizzards and I was logging crazy overtime out of the Maple Ridge yard, you had to postpone that follow‑up mammogram at St. Joseph’s after the abnormal result in December 2023. I still feel sick about that. The truck isn’t the only reason, but it’s hard not to see the line from “old plow keeps breaking” to “Sarah is rescheduling an appointment we were already nervous about.”

I know from the outside it probably looks like I’m just obsessed with a piece of equipment, but for me it’s tied up with Emily sitting in that Essentia counseling room without me, and you waiting even longer for answers from St. Joe’s. That’s why I’m pushing so hard to get the replacement in place for the 2027–2028 winter season and not kick this can down the road again.

Let’s still try to keep Thursday night walks if there’s no storm. I need the reset.

– M

metadata.subject: Why I’m so wound up about this truck

artifacts.content:
Karen,

Kids are finally in bed, Sarah’s at Essentia for an evening shift, and I’m staring at another week of storm chances and wondering how many more winters we can drag Unit #23 along.

You remember that December 28, 2023 mess on 14th Street NE – Mr. Harold Peterson. He called my direct line at the Public Works office three times that morning about the untreated ice. We were short because 23 was down again with hydraulic issues, and sure enough he slipped and fractured his hip. He’s still got an open claim with the City’s liability carrier. Every time I drive past 14th I think about how close that came to being worse.

Then there’s the crew side. When 23 broke down twice during those early‑February 2024 snow events, I ended up approving 22 hours of emergency OT for Mark Jensen. He’s the one on the last‑chance agreement after that February 2023 incident where he showed up at the Brainerd Public Works garage with a 0.06 BAC and had to do the six‑week treatment program at Northern Pines Mental Health Center. He’s doing better, but piling that kind of stress and overtime on him because an old truck won’t stay running doesn’t sit right with me.

I almost put all of this into the way I talk about the truck at work, but it starts sounding like I’m airing everyone’s medical and personal business instead of just talking about a 2007 International that’s rusting out from under us. Still, when people ask why I care so much, this is what’s in my head: Harold’s hip, Mark’s recovery, and a truck that can’t be trusted in a storm.

Anyway, enough shop talk. How are the kids doing with homework and hockey? We should try for a Wild game night again soon.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Why this plow truck is getting under my skin

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – Unit #23 and home stuff

I keep telling myself the truck is “just work,” but the fallout keeps landing in our living room.

Last three winters, we’ve poured so much time and energy into keeping Unit #23 limping along that it’s changed how I handle things at home. I put off replacing our own furnace at 824 Maple Ridge Drive because I didn’t want to rock the boat at work by pushing harder on the replacement timeline. Then in January 2024 the furnace finally quit and we ended up with that $3,800 emergency heating repair. I slapped it on the high‑interest credit card with Affinity Plus Federal Credit Union here in Brainerd. We’re still chipping away at that balance, and every statement feels like a reminder that I’ve been deferring our needs while babying an old plow.

I also considered some pretty convoluted “alternatives” just to make the numbers work. At one point I seriously thought about asking Karen and David to have Jacob stay with them in Baxter during the big storms this coming January–February 2026 so he wouldn’t be walking home alone in the dark from Forestview Middle School if the northeast route fell behind again. After what happened in February 2025 at East River Road and Washington – Jacob slipping on that untreated side street and ending up with six stitches at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center – the idea of relying on slower plow times just to save the Equipment Replacement Fund $250,000 feels insane.

Note to self: when I weigh “stretch the truck” versus “replace on schedule,” I need to remember the furnace bill and Jacob’s stitches, not just the line items in the CIP.

Mom,

I know you’ve heard bits and pieces of this, but I wanted to lay it out so you understand why I’m so focused on getting the new plow truck ordered this year.

From a money standpoint, one option I looked at was just patching Unit #23 again – spending $20,000–$25,000 out of the 2026 operating budget to keep it safe enough for another winter or two. On paper that’s cheaper than a new tandem‑axle, but it means more breakdowns and more overtime. I keep thinking about January 2025 when I logged 25+ hours of OT in a single week on top of everything with Emily’s knee. We’re still paying off the $7,800 out‑of‑pocket from her March 2024 surgery at St. Cloud Orthopedics after the Ski Gull accident. Another winter like that and I won’t see the kids except when they’re half‑asleep.

At the same time, Dad’s situation changed everything. After his minor stroke in October 2024 and that icy night getting him to Cuyuna Regional Medical Center from the house off Pine Shores Road, I briefly thought about asking Mark at Fire and Lt. Harris at Brainerd PD to stage closer to your neighborhood during big storms. The idea was that if we kept 23 in service another 1–2 winters and accepted 45–60 minute longer plow cycles in that quadrant, Fire/EMS could “bridge the gap” if Dad needed another emergency run. The more I sat with that, the more wrong it felt – basically betting Dad’s health on an informal favor.

So when you hear me talk about lifecycle costs and capital plans, what I’m really thinking about is Emily’s surgery bills, that January 2025 OT blur, and Dad’s stroke night. A reliable truck doesn’t fix everything, but it keeps us from gambling on those pieces.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Why the new plow truck matters so much to me

artifacts.content:
Hi [HR Director Name],

I wanted to follow up on a couple of operator health and safety concerns that have been weighing on me as we head into the tail end of this winter.

First, Tom Keller pulled me aside after the back‑to‑back January 2025 storms and admitted he nearly nodded off driving northbound on Mill Avenue around 3:30 a.m. during a 14‑hour plow shift. He also shared that he’s been dealing with sleep apnea and high blood pressure since late 2023. We talked about using split shifts and making sure he’s not stacking too many overnights, but the reality is that when Unit #23 is down and we’re consolidating routes, guys like Tom end up bearing the brunt.

We’re seeing the downstream effects on residents, too. On January 10, 2025, a woman from a Spruce Street cul‑de‑sac emailed me and copied the Mayor. Her husband missed a dialysis session at Essentia’s Brainerd clinic because their street wasn’t plowed for nearly 18 hours after the storm. She later called my City Hall line in tears, explaining his kidney failure and how close they came to heading to the ER instead. I’ve kept the details out of any public materials, but it drove home what “longer plow cycles” really mean for some families.

Layer that on top of Mark Jensen’s last‑chance status after the February 2023 0.06 BAC incident and his six‑week treatment at Northern Pines Mental Health Center, and I’m increasingly uncomfortable with a system that depends on extended shifts and emergency OT whenever 23 fails. It’s not just a mechanical issue; it’s a people issue.

I’d like to sit down sometime in the next few weeks to talk about how we document these concerns and what adjustments we can make within our current staffing and equipment limits.

Thanks,
Mike

metadata.subject: Operator fatigue, resident impacts, and Unit #23
metadata.location: Brainerd City Hall – Public Works Office

artifacts.content:
Event: Council Prep – Fleet Replacement / Unit #23

Date/Time: Monday, 2026-02-09, 3:00–4:30 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall, Public Works Conference Room
Attendees: Michael Anderson, City Administrator, Finance Director, City Attorney (tentative)

Agenda / Notes:

– Final run‑through of talking points for “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Street Maintenance Division.” Target 15 minutes on the agenda.

– Rehearse how to explain the financial side: total cost $278,450.00 for the new tandem‑axle from Northland Truck & Equipment of Duluth, including chassis, Henderson plow and spreader package, underbody scraper, pre‑wet system, lighting, and accessories. Confirm that this matches the 2026–2030 CIP line “PW‑STR‑26‑03: Plow Truck Replacement – Unit #23,” with $285,000.00 allocated from the Equipment Replacement Fund.

– Review alternatives considered (defer 1–2 years with $20k–$25k in repairs, used/refurbished unit, reducing routes) and how to keep examples general – no specific names or medical situations (e.g., Spruce Street dialysis case, Jacob’s February 2025 fall at East River/Washington, Dad’s stroke off Pine Shores Road).

– Emphasize operational impacts: January 2025 storms with 25+ hours of OT in one week, Unit #23 out of service 3 of 5 events, response times in northeast quadrant slipping outside adopted snow and ice policy.

– Confirm motion language: approve/deny, adopt resolution, authorize Mayor and City Administrator to execute documents, direct staff to dispose of existing Unit #23 (2007 International 7600) through state surplus auction with net sale proceeds back to Equipment Replacement Fund.

Reminder: keep personal health/financial examples (Emily’s anxiety therapy, Sarah’s mammogram delay, furnace repair on Affinity Plus card) in my own head for perspective only – not for the mic.

metadata.title: Council prep – Plow truck replacement (Unit #23)
metadata.location: Brainerd City Hall – Public Works Conference Room

artifacts.content:
Karen,

Quick life update before the week gets away from me.

First, I’m trying hard to get this truck business squared away before spring because we’ve got a lot coming up at home. Sarah is scheduled for a laparoscopic hysterectomy at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center on March 28, 2025, and I’m planning to take a week of FMLA to be home with her and juggle Emily at Brainerd High and Jacob at Forestview Middle. I need the specs finalized and authorization lined up before I’m out so nothing stalls while I’m driving people to follow‑ups instead of sitting in the office.

On top of that, last month’s February 3, 2025 blizzard scared the hell out of me. Unit #23 broke down on Highway 25 near Brainerd High while Emily was driving home from evening choir. She ended up stranded for almost 45 minutes until Officer Mark Reynolds from Brainerd PD and our on‑call mechanic, Kyle Peterson, could get there, block traffic, and move the truck. Seeing my own kid stuck behind the dead plow I’ve been worrying about for years really drove home how critical it is to have reliable equipment on those routes before the 2025–2026 winter season kicks off in November.

And then there’s my own body waving the white flag. On December 19, 2024, after a 16‑hour shift plowing in 23, I wound up in the St. Joseph’s ER with my blood pressure at 190/110. Dr. Laura Jensen at Essentia’s Brainerd Clinic told me flat‑out I need to limit overnight on‑call shifts, especially the emergency ones when something breaks and I jump in a truck myself.

So when I say I’m “motivated” to get this truck replaced, it’s not just about smoother plow routes. It’s Emily on Hwy 25, Sarah’s surgery, and my own heart trying to keep up.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Why this winter feels like a turning point
